title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Leopold Reynolds tells Hercule Poirot that Joyce likely fabricated her claim of witnessing a murder to gain attention, noting that Beatrice and Kitty laughed at her story. Poirot then questions Joyce's sister, Anne, who confirms she heard the claim but did not take it seriously. Anne explains that Joyce was a habitual liar who frequently exaggerated stories, such as a trip to India, to impress others. Anne further notes that while there have been deaths in the area, no unusual murder cases have occurred in Woodleigh for several years.

Later, at Scone Manor, Poirot dines with Inspector Spence and Elspeth McKay. Elspeth characterizes Joyce as a "master of deception" who told outrageous stories to seek attention. While she acknowledges that Joyce might have seen something, she remains skeptical of the girl's credibility. When Poirot inquires about potential victims from the last three years, Spence provides a list of names: Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith, Charlotte Belfield, Janet White, and Leslie Firrel.

Poirot becomes interested in Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith. Elspeth mentions a foreign student who lived with an old lady and disappeared shortly after the woman's death. Although Spence suggests the student ran off with a man, Poirot questions the lack of rumors regarding the man's identity. While the death of Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith was attributed to regular heart trouble, Poirot questions why Spence placed her at the top of his list of potential victims.
